analogy: Think of "Reproduction in Organisms" through the chapter's own opening images: Biology in essence is the story of life on earth. While individual organisms die without fail, species continue to live through millions of years unless threatened by natural or anthropogenic extinction. Reproduction becomes a vital process without which species cannot survive for long. Each individual... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Reproduction in Organisms is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that biology in essence is the story of life on earth.

The text explains that while individual organisms die without fail, species continue to live through millions of years unless threatened by natural or anthropogenic extinction.

The text explains that reproduction becomes a vital process without which species cannot survive for long.

The text explains that each individual leaves its progeny by asexual or sexual means.

The text explains that sexual mode of reproduction enables creation of new variants, so that survival advantage is enhanced.

The text explains that this unit explains the details of reproductive processes in flowering plants and humans as easy to relate representative examples.

The text explains that a related perspective on human reproductive health and how reproductive ill health can be avoided is also presented to complete our understanding of biology of reproduction.

The text explains that chapter 1 Sexual Reproduction in flowering Plants Chapter 2 Human Reproduction Chapter 3 Reproductive Health

The text explains that pANCHANAN MAHESHWARI (1904-1966) Born in November 1904 in Jaipur (Rajasthan) Panchanan Maheshwari rose to become one of the most distinguished botanists not only of India but of the entire world.

The text explains that he moved to Allahabad for higher education where he obtained his D.Sc.

The text explains that during his college days, he was inspired by Dr W.

The text explains that dudgeon, an American missionary teacher, to develop interest in Botany and especially morphology.

The text explains that his teacher once expressed that if his student progresses ahead of him, it will give him a great satisfaction.

The text explains that these words encouraged Panchanan to enquire what he could do for his teacher in return.

The text explains that he worked on embryological aspects and popularised the use of embryological characters in taxonomy.

The text explains that he established the Department of Botany, University of Delhi as an important centre of research in embryology and tissue culture.

The text explains that it can withstand high temperatures and strong acids and alkali.

The text explains that the period for which pollen grains remain viable is highly variable and to some extent depends on the prevailing temperature and humidity.

The text explains that kinds of Pollination : Depending on the source of pollen, pollination can be divided into three types.

The text explains that wind- pollinated flowers often have a single ovule in each ovary and numerous flowers packed into an inflorescence; a familiar example is the corn cob – the ears you see are nothing but the stigma and style which wave in the wind to trap pollen grains.

The text explains that the embryo may enter a state of inactivity called dormancy, or if favourable conditions are available (adequate moisture, oxygen and suitable temperature), they germinate.

The text explains that 25 HUMAN REPRODUCTION SEXUAL REPRODUCTION IN FLOWERING PLANTS 7.

The text explains that can cross-pollination occur in cleistogamous flowers?

The text explains that mention two strategies evolved to prevent self-pollination in flowers.

The text explains that why does self-pollination not lead to seed formation in self-incompatible species?

The text explains that how is it useful in a plant breeding programme?

The text explains that why do you think the zygote is dormant for sometime in a fertilised ovule?

The text explains that differentiate between: (a) hypocotyl and epicotyl; (b) coleoptile and coleorrhiza; (c) integument and testa; (d) perisperm and pericarp.

The text explains that when and why does a plant breeder employ this technique?

The text explains that if one can induce parthenocarpy through the application of growth substances, which fruits would you select to induce parthenocarpy and why?

The text explains that explain the role of tapetum in the formation of pollen-grain wall.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Biology, Chapter 1: Reproduction in Organisms

prelims_traps: Do not treat PANCHANAN MAHESHWARI and 1.1 FLOWER – A FASCINATING ORGAN OF as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Reproduction in Organisms; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Reproduction in Organisms through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use PANCHANAN MAHESHWARI and 1.1 FLOWER – A FASCINATING ORGAN OF to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
